channel.title: Property-Access on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Property-Access on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property 'X' does not exist on type 'Y'
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ does not exist on type ‘Y’

TS2339 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that does not define it. TypeScript’s type system enforces that you can only access properties that exist on the declared type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing non-existent property
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.email); // TS2339

// Cause 2: Wrong type annotation
const data: string = "hello";
data.length; // OK
data.toFixed(2); // TS2339: string has no toFixed

// Cause 3: Using API response without type definition
const response = fetch("/api/user");
const data = await response.json();
console.log(data.user.name); // TS2339: 'user' does not exist

How to Fix

Fix 1: Add missing property to interface

interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
 email: string;
}

Fix 2: Use optional chaining

console.log(user?.email);

Fix 3: Type the API response

interface ApiResponse {
 user: { name: string; age: number };
}
const data: ApiResponse = await response.json();
console.log(data.user.name);

Fix 4: Use type assertion or index signature

interface StringMap {
 [key: string]: string;
}
const map: StringMap = { name: "Alice" };
console.log(map["name"]);

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property is type-only import — type-only import access.
  • TS2551: Property does not exist - did you mean — typo suggestions.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required property.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 — Property 'X' is a type-only import
item.description:
TS2339 — Property ‘X’ is a type-only import

TS2339 can occur when you try to use a type-only import as a value. With verbatimModuleSyntax or isolatedModules, TypeScript enforces that type-only imports cannot be used at runtime.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using type import as value
import type { User } from "./types";
const u: User = { name: "Alice" }; // OK as type annotation
const u2 = new User(); // TS2339: User is type-only

// Cause 2: Re-exporting type as value
import type { Config } from "./config";
export { Config }; // TS2339: Config is type-only

// Cause 3: Import without 'type' used as type-only
import { Logger } from "./logger";
// Logger is only used as type, but imported as value

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use import type correctly

import type { User } from "./types";
// Can only use User as a type
const u: User = { name: "Alice" };

Fix 2: Separate value and type imports

import { helper } from "./utils";
import type { Config } from "./config";

Fix 3: Use inline type import

import { helper, type Config } from "./module";

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — regular property access.
  • TS2693: ‘X’ only refers to a type — type used as value.
  • TS2307: Cannot find module — module not found.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2339 v2 — Property Does Not Exist on Type Fix
item.description:
TS2339 — Property Does Not Exist on Type (v2)

This variant of TS2339 covers situations where property access fails on union types, generic constraints, or when working with third-party library types that lack proper typings.

What This Error Means

Common error messages:

  • TS2339: Property 'name' does not exist on type 'User | null'
  • TS2339: Property 'data' does not exist on type 'unknown'
  • TS2339: Property 'map' does not exist on type 'T'
  • TS2339: Property 'toISOString' does not exist on type 'string | Date'

TypeScript prevents accessing properties that the current type annotation doesn’t include. This often happens after narrowing, with union types, or when the type is inferred as unknown.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2459 — Type 'X' has no property 'Y'
item.description:
TS2459 — Type ‘X’ has no property ‘Y’ and no string index signature

TS2459 occurs when you try to access a property on a type that doesn’t have it and doesn’t have an index signature that would allow arbitrary property access.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing property on wrong type
type StringOrNumber = string | number;
const val: StringOrNumber = "hello";
val.toFixed(2); // TS2459: 'string | number' has no property 'toFixed'

// Cause 2: Missing index signature
interface User {
 name: string;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice" };
user["email"] = "test"; // TS2459: no string index signature

// Cause 3: Union type property access
type A = { x: number };
type B = { y: number };
const val: A | B = { x: 1 };
val.x; // TS2459: 'y' is not common

How to Fix

Fix 1: Narrow the type first

if (typeof val === "number") {
 val.toFixed(2); // OK
}

Fix 2: Add index signature

interface User {
 name: string;
 [key: string]: string;
}

Fix 3: Use discriminated union

type A = { kind: "a"; x: number };
type B = { kind: "b"; y: number };
function handle(val: A | B) {
 if (val.kind === "a") {
 console.log(val.x);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2345: Argument not assignable — type mismatch in call.
  • TS2322: Type not assignable — assignment type mismatch.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2542 — Cannot Read Property of Type
item.description:
TS2542 — Cannot read property ‘X’ of type ‘Y’

TS2542 occurs when you try to read a property from a type that doesn’t allow property access, typically with Readonly<T> types or when accessing properties on types that lack index signatures. This is less common than TS2540 and usually involves specific readonly constraints.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing property on wrong type
interface ReadonlyUser {
 readonly name: string;
 readonly age: number;
}
const user: ReadonlyUser = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
const data: ReadonlyUser = user;
// TS2542 if you try to access data on a type that doesn't expose it

// Cause 2: Indexed access on a readonly tuple
const tuple: readonly [string, number] = ["hello", 42];
const val: string = tuple[0]; // OK
tuple[0] = "world"; // TS2540 (write), not TS2542

// Cause 3: Using 'in' operator on type without property
type Empty = {};
const x: Empty = {};
"prop" in x; // No error, but...

// Cause 4: Readonly mapped type access
type ReadonlyObj = Readonly<{ x: number; y: number }>;
const obj: ReadonlyObj = { x: 1, y: 2 };

Solutions

Fix 1: Use the correct type that allows property access

// If you need to read properties, ensure the type exposes them
interface User {
 name: string;
 age: number;
}
const user: User = { name: "Alice", age: 30 };
console.log(user.name); // OK

Fix 2: Use a mutable type when mutation is needed

// Wrong — readonly prevents writes
type ReadonlyUser = Readonly<{ name: string }>;

// Correct — use mutable type for mutable operations
type User = { name: string };

Fix 3: Cast to a type with the needed property

interface Base {
 id: number;
}
interface Extended extends Base {
 name: string;
}
const obj: Base = { id: 1 };
(obj as Extended).name; // Access via assertion

Fix 4: Use type narrowing for conditional access

function getValue(obj: unknown) {
 if (typeof obj === "object" && obj !== null && "name" in obj) {
 return (obj as { name: string }).name; // Safe access
 }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Readonly array methods
const arr: readonly number[] = [1, 2, 3];
arr.filter(x => x > 1); // OK — read-only methods allowed
arr.sort(); // TS2542 — mutating method not allowed on readonly array

// Example 2: Frozen object
const frozen = Object.freeze({ config: { debug: true } });
frozen.config = { debug: false }; // TS2542 / TS2540

Related Errors

  • TS2540: Cannot assign to read-only property — write access on readonly.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — property not declared.
  • TS2588: Cannot assign to ‘const’ variable — const assignment.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2551 — Property 'X' does not exist - did you mean 'Y'?
item.description:
TS2551 — Property ‘X’ does not exist - did you mean ‘Y’?

TS2551 is an enhanced version of TS2339 where TypeScript detects a property access that looks like a typo and suggests a similar property name that does exist on the type.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Typo in property name
interface User {
 firstName: string;
 lastName: string;
}
const user: User = { firstName: "Alice", lastName: "Smith" };
console.log(user.firsName); // TS2551: Did you mean 'firstName'?

// Cause 2: Wrong casing
const el = document.getElementById("myId");
el.innerHtml = "hello"; // TS2551: Did you mean 'innerHTML'?

// Cause 3: Missing import from destructured object
const { namee } = { name: "Alice" }; // TS2551: Did you mean 'name'?

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use the suggested property name

console.log(user.firstName); // correct spelling

Fix 2: Use the IDE suggestion

TypeScript language servers show quick fixes — accept the suggestion to rename automatically.

item.title: PHP Warning: Trying to get property of non-object
item.description:
PHP Warning: Trying to get property of non-object

This warning occurs when you try to access a property on a variable that is not an object. In PHP 7.4+, this produces a warning; in PHP 8.0+, accessing a property on null throws a fatal error.

Common Causes

  • Variable is null instead of an expected object (e.g., failed database query)
  • Function returns null or false instead of an object
  • Array was passed where an object was expected
  • Object not properly initialized before property access

How to Fix

Check Variable Type Before Access

<?php
$user = getUserFromDatabase($id);
if (is_object($user)) {
 $name = $user->name;
}
?>

Use the Null Operator (PHP 7.4+)

<?php
$name = $user?->name ?? 'default';
?>

Verify Database Query Results

<?php
$result = $db->query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 1");
$row = $result->fetchObject();
if ($row) {
 echo $row->name;
}
?>

Use instanceof Check

<?php
if ($obj instanceof User) {
 echo $obj->getFullName();
}
?>

Examples

<?php
// Example 1: Null variable
$user = null;
echo $user->name;
// Warning: Trying to get property 'name' of non-object
// Fix: check if user is not null first

// Example 2: Array passed instead of object
$config = ['host' => 'localhost'];
echo $config->host;
// Warning: Trying to get property 'host' of non-object
// Fix: use $config['host'] instead

// Example 3: Failed query
$result = $db->query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 999");
$user = $result->fetchObject();
echo $user->name;
// Warning: Trying to get property 'name' of non-object (user is false)
// Fix: if ($user) { echo $user->name; }
?>

Related Errors

  • PHP Notice: Undefined Variable
  • PHP Fatal error: Call to undefined function
  • PHP Fatal Error: Allowed memory size exhausted
